abstract: What does it take to build a visual reasoner that works across charts, science, spatial understanding, and open-ended tasks? The strongest vision-language models (VLMs) show such broad visual reasoning is within reach, but the recipe behind them remains unclear, locked behind proprietary reinforcement learning (RL) pipelines with non-public data. We introduce Vero, a family of fully open VLMs that matches or exceeds existing open-weight models across diverse visual reasoning tasks. We scale RL data and rewards across six broad task categories, constructing Vero-600K, a 600K-sample dataset from 59 datasets, and designing task-routed rewards that handle heterogeneous answer formats. Vero achieves state-of-the-art performance, improving over four base models by 3.7-5.5 points on average across VeroEval, our suite of 30 challenging benchmarks. Starting from Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct, Vero outperforms Qwen3-VL-8B-Thinking on 23 of 30 benchmarks without additional proprietary thinking data. When trained from the same base model, Vero-600K exceeds existing RL datasets across task categories. Systematic ablations reveal that different task categories elicit qualitatively distinct reasoning patterns that transfer poorly in isolation, suggesting that broad data coverage is the primary driver of strong RL scaling. All data, code, and models are released.

citations.fullCitation: Zheng, Chujie, Shixuan Liu, Mingze Li, Xiong-Hui Chen, Bowen Yu, Chang Gao, Kai Dang, Yuqiong Liu, Rui Men, An Yang, et al. Group sequence policy optimization. arXiv preprint arXiv:2507.18071, 2025a.
citations.justification: This paper introduces Group Sequence Policy Optimization (GSPO), the core reinforcement learning algorithm used to train Vero. The main paper explicitly states its method integrates advances from GSPO and includes an ablation study showing that GSPO outperforms other RL algorithms like GRPO, making it a foundational component of the Vero recipe.
citations.title: Group sequence policy optimization

citations.fullCitation: Shuai Bai, Yuxuan Cai, Ruizhe Chen, Keqin Chen, Xionghui Chen, Zesen Cheng, Lianghao Deng, Wei Ding, Chang Gao, Chunjiang Ge, et al. Qwen3-vl technical report. arXiv preprint arXiv:2511.21631, 2025a.
citations.justification: This report details the Qwen3-VL models, which serve as the primary base models for training Vero. The main performance claims of the Vero paper are centered on significantly improving over the Qwen3-VL-Instruct and outperforming the Qwen3-VL-Thinking variants, making this a crucial reference for understanding the starting point and key comparison.
citations.title: Qwen3-vl technical report

citations.fullCitation: Kaichen Zhang, Keming Wu, Zuhao Yang, Bo Li, Kairui Hu, Bin Wang, Ziwei Liu, Xingxuan Li, and Lidong Bing. Openmmreasoner: Pushing the frontiers for multimodal reasoning with an open and general recipe. In CVPR, 2026.
citations.justification: This work is a key example of a prior fully open RL recipe for VLMs, but with a narrow focus on visual math. The Vero paper repeatedly contrasts its own broad, multi-domain approach with the specialized scope of OpenMMReasoner to highlight its main contribution of achieving general visual reasoning across diverse tasks.
citations.title: Openmmreasoner: Pushing the frontiers for multimodal reasoning with an open and general recipe

citations.fullCitation: Haozhe Wang, Chao Qu, Zuming Huang, Wei Chu, Fangzhen Lin, and Wenhu Chen. VL-Rethinker: Incentivizing self-reflection of vision-language models with reinforcement learning. In NeurIPS, 2025.
citations.justification: This paper is cited as a significant prior work on fully open RL for VLMs and is used as a direct baseline in performance comparisons. The Vero paper argues that its broad data coverage allows it to generalize better than narrowly-trained models like VL-Rethinker, making this a key point of comparison to establish state-of-the-art performance.
citations.title: VL-Rethinker: Incentivizing self-reflection of vision-language models with reinforcement learning

intermediateReport:
The following report provides a detailed analysis of the research paper "Vero: An Open RL Recipe for General Visual Reasoning."

---

## Detailed Report: Vero: An Open RL Recipe for General Visual Reasoning

### 1. Authors and Institution(s)

The research was conducted by Gabriel Sarch, Linrong Cai, Qunzhong Wang, Haoyang Wu, Danqi Chen, and Zhuang Liu, all affiliated with Princeton University. Gabriel Sarch and Linrong Cai are noted as project leads, and Zhuang Liu as the corresponding author.

### 2. How This Work Fits into the Broader Research Landscape

Vision-language models (VLMs) are increasingly designed to perform a wide array of visual tasks, encompassing chart interpretation, scientific analysis, spatial understanding, and open-ended question answering. Reinforcement Learning (RL) has emerged as a significant factor in advancing multimodal reasoning capabilities in these models, with techniques such as Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) and Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) enabling models to learn from their own generated responses via reward signals.

Current state-of-the-art multimodal reasoning models, including proprietary systems like GPT-5 and Kimi K2.5, as well as open-weight families such as Qwen3-VL, have demonstrated substantial performance improvements driven by RL. However, the methodologies underlying the RL pipelines of these high-performing models often remain undisclosed, with proprietary data and non-public reward designs. This lack of transparency limits the ability of the broader research community to systematically analyze design choices, diagnose failure modes, and attribute performance gains to specific components.

In contrast, existing fully open-source RL efforts, such as OpenMMReasoner and VL-Rethinker, have primarily concentrated on narrow domains, such as visual mathematics. The present work empirically demonstrates that training on such restricted task categories does not typically generalize effectively to a wider spectrum of visual reasoning capabilities. Furthermore, applying RL across a diverse range of heterogeneous visual reasoning tasks presents challenges, including potential interference, weak transfer of learned skills, and optimization imbalances, unless the training data distribution and reward mechanisms are carefully structured.

This research addresses these gaps by introducing Vero, an initiative to provide a fully open RL recipe for general visual understanding. The work aims to elucidate the necessary components for training a broadly capable visual reasoner, offering a transparent and reproducible framework that seeks to match or exceed the performance of existing open-weight models across a diverse set of visual reasoning tasks.

### 3. Key Objectives and Motivation

The central objective of this research is to determine the requirements for constructing a visual reasoner capable of performing effectively across a broad range of visual tasks, including interpreting charts, scientific figures, spatial configurations, and responding to open-ended queries. This overarching goal is driven by several motivations:

*   **Addressing Transparency Deficits:** The most advanced visual reasoning models frequently result from proprietary RL pipelines that utilize non-public data and undisclosed reward designs. This opacity prevents the scientific community from conducting detailed ablations, understanding the precise mechanisms driving performance improvements, and diagnosing limitations. Vero seeks to counter this by providing a fully open recipe, including data, code, and models, to foster reproducibility and mechanistic understanding.
*   **Overcoming Domain-Specific Limitations of Open Efforts:** Existing open RL initiatives for VLMs have predominantly focused on narrow domains, such as visual mathematics or specific perceptual tasks. The authors demonstrate empirically that training confined to a single task category often fails to generalize effectively to other visual capabilities, resulting in poor transfer of reasoning patterns. A core motivation is to develop an RL approach that facilitates broad generalization rather than narrow specialization.
*   **Managing Challenges of Multi-Task RL:** Applying RL across a heterogeneous mix of visual reasoning tasks is inherently complex. Such diverse task mixtures can lead to interference among learned skills, weak transferability between domains, and imbalances during optimization. The research is motivated by the need to design a training distribution and reward system that can mitigate these challenges, enabling positive transfer and robust performance across various tasks.
*   **Systematic Investigation of Design Choices:** The project aims to systematically investigate and validate the impact of various design choices in multi-task RL for VLMs. This includes examining dataset selection, sample filtering techniques, data mixture strategies, and the design of reward functions. By providing detailed ablations, the authors intend to clarify which factors are critical for scaling RL to general visual reasoning.
*   **Enhancing Generalization and Visual Chat Ability:** A specific objective is to develop a VLM that can not only achieve high accuracy on diverse benchmarks but also maintain or improve its open-ended visual chat capabilities, which can sometimes degrade during RL training focused on structured answers.

In summary, the motivation is to demystify and open the development of general visual reasoners trained with RL, providing a robust, transparent, and broadly applicable recipe that advances multimodal AI capabilities.

### 4. Methodology and Approach

The Vero project's methodology centers on a single-stage reinforcement learning (RL) recipe applied to existing base vision-language models (VLMs), designed to achieve strong performance across diverse visual reasoning tasks. The approach is underpinned by careful dataset curation, effective data mixing strategies, and a multi-faceted reward system.

**A. Vero-600K Dataset Construction**

Vero-600K is a 600,000-sample training dataset derived from 59 distinct datasets, categorized into six broad task categories. This categorization is motivated by empirical observations that these categories elicit distinct reasoning patterns and that training on a single category does not transfer reliably to others. The six categories are:
1.  **STEM:** Mathematical diagram reasoning, scientific figure interpretation, medical image understanding.
2.  **Spatial & Action:** Embodied reasoning, UI navigation, 3D spatial understanding, action sequences.
3.  **Knowledge & Recognition:** Visual question answering combining object/scene/entity recognition with external or commonsense knowledge.
4.  **Chart & OCR:** Extracting and reasoning over structured information in documents, charts, tables, infographics.
5.  **Grounding, Counting & Search:** Spatially localizing objects, counting instances, visual search among distractors.
6.  **Captioning & Instruction Following:** Open-ended image description and adherence to prompt instructions.

The data curation pipeline involves multiple stages:
1.  **Dataset Sourcing and Selection:** Over 250 candidate datasets were initially considered.
    *   **Heuristic Selection:** Datasets with fewer than 1,000 examples, average image resolution below 200,000 pixels (with five exceptions for question quality), or binary questions were discarded to mitigate guessing and ensure sufficient data quality.
    *   **Manual Selection:** 59 datasets were retained after manual inspection of approximately 50 examples per candidate against three criteria: correctness (<5% annotation error), unambiguity (single verifiable answer), and verifiability (compatibility with reward functions). Some questions were rewritten for clarity, or high-error subsets were dropped.
2.  **Data Filtering (Individual Samples):**
    *   **Question Filtering:** Qwen3-VL-235B-A22B-Instruct was employed to identify and remove ambiguous, image-irrelevant, or unverifiable questions based on five criteria: relevance, ambiguity, language, verifiability, and numeric precision.
    *   **Answer Filtering:** A text-only Qwen3-235B-A22B-Instruct model was used to normalize ground-truth answers (e.g., stripping units, converting to decimals, normalizing multiple-choice letters) to ensure stable reward computation. Samples with multi-value answers, non-reducible symbolic expressions, or ambiguous semantic descriptions were removed, except for the Captioning & Instruction Following category.
3.  **Data Mixtures:** The study investigated various weighting schemes for combining task categories in the training data: uniform, difficulty-weighted (inverse accuracy), image-area-weighted (mean input resolution), and reasoning-length-weighted (mean chain-of-thought length). Uniform weighting across task categories was found to achieve the best overall performance.

**B. VeroEval Evaluation Suite**

A comprehensive evaluation suite, VeroEval, was assembled, comprising 30 challenging benchmarks across the six defined visual reasoning categories. Benchmarks were selected based on three criteria: difficulty (leaving room for improvement for frontier models), annotation quality (well-defined protocols and reliable labels), and intra-category diversity (testing complementary sub-skills within each category).

**C. Reinforcement Learning Training**
1.  **Algorithmic Details:** The RL algorithm is based on Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), incorporating advancements from GSPO (Group Sequence Policy Optimization). GSPO replaces per-token importance ratios with a sequence-level ratio, forming a token-level ratio from the sequence-average log-probability difference. The objective function is a clipped surrogate loss, utilizing asymmetric clipping (ε_high > ε_low) and no KL penalty to allow for less restricted policy updates. A soft overlong penalty is applied to discourage excessively long responses.
2.  **Reward Formulation:** The total reward R(y, y*) combines three components:
    *   **Accuracy Reward (R_acc):** This is the primary component and is task-routed, employing ten distinct reward functions tailored to specific answer types (e.g., string match, multiple choice, numeric, list string match, ordering, web action, grounding, clicking, instruction following, LLM-as-judge). For tasks blending programmatic instruction following with open-ended judgment, a blended accuracy score (weighted average of programmatic checks and LLM-as-judge scores) is used.
    *   **Format Reward (R_fmt):** Penalizes responses that deviate from the `<think>...</think><answer>...</answer>` format or misuse the `\boxed{...}` notation for discrete answers (e.g., its absence when required or presence of multiple boxed expressions).
    *   **Overlong Penalty (R_overlong):** A linear ramp penalty that discourages responses exceeding a maximum token length.
    *   **LLM-as-Judge:** For open-ended tasks, a Qwen3-32B model (with thinking disabled) acts as a judge, scoring responses on a 1-10 scale. Strict guidelines and "Automatic Failure Conditions" (e.g., penalizing self-evaluative language or fabricated details) are implemented to mitigate reward hacking.
3.  **Base Models:** Vero models were trained from four different base VLMs: Qwen2.5-VL-7B-Instruct, Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct, Qwen3-VL-8B-Thinking, and MiMo-VL-7B-SFT.

### 5. Main Findings and Results

The research presents several findings regarding the performance, robustness, and underlying mechanisms of Vero models.

**A. State-of-the-Art Performance with a Fully Open RL Recipe**
*   The models Vero-Qwen3T-8B and Vero-Qwen3I-8B achieved the highest overall average scores of 65.9 and 66.0, respectively, among all evaluated 8B-parameter VLMs on the 30 benchmarks in VeroEval. These models demonstrated superior performance across all six task categories compared to baseline models.
*   Specifically, Vero-Qwen3T-8B surpassed its base model, Qwen3-VL-8B-Thinking, on 24 out of 30 benchmarks, with notable gains such as +8.6 points on Grounding, Counting & Search, and +4.2 points on Chart & OCR. Vero-Qwen3I-8B similarly outperformed Qwen3-VL-8B-Thinking on 23 out of 30 benchmarks.
*   Against other fully open RL-trained models, Vero variants consistently showed superior performance, outperforming LLaVA-OV-1.5-RL on 10 out of 10 overlapping benchmarks and Molmo2-O-7B on 28 out of 29 overlaps.

**B. Consistent Gains Across Base Models, Beating Proprietary Recipes**
*   Vero training consistently yielded performance improvements across four different base models, with average gains ranging from +3.7 to +5.5 points across the 30 benchmarks.
*   Vero-Qwen25-7B, starting from a comparatively weaker base model (Qwen2.5-VL-7B-Instruct), managed to surpass Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct on two category averages (Chart & OCR, and Knowledge & Recognition), illustrating the effectiveness of the RL recipe in bridging significant base model performance gaps.
*   Vero-MiMo-7B, trained using the open Vero recipe on MiMo-VL-7B-SFT, outperformed MiMo-VL-7B-RL (which was trained with a proprietary RL recipe and non-public data) on 3 of 6 category averages (STEM, Knowledge & Recognition, and Captioning & Instruction Following), suggesting that the open recipe can yield competitive or superior results compared to closed proprietary systems.

**C. Improvements Not Limited to a Single Domain**
*   Unlike many prior open RL-trained VLMs that specialize in specific domains (e.g., visual math), Vero demonstrated substantial improvements across *all six* task categories. This broad enhancement, with gains such as +15.9 on ChartQA Pro, +15.1 on MMMU-Pro Vision, and +13.6 on ScreenSpotPro, indicates that the multi-task RL approach successfully produces generally capable models rather than narrow specialists.

**D. Consistent Advantage Over Prior Open RL Datasets Across Training**
*   Comparing training curves, Vero-600K consistently outperformed or matched three prior open RL datasets (ViRL-39k, OpenMMReasoner-74k, and LLaVA-OV-1.5-RL-70k) when training the same base model. Vero-600K maintained its lead on five out of six categories throughout the training process, with particularly large margins on Captioning & Instruction Following (+15.0 points) and Grounding, Counting & Search (+5.9 points).

**E. Ablation Studies**
*   **Reward Design:** A multi-route reward design, which employs task-specific comparisons, significantly outperformed a unified `math_verify` baseline. The multi-route design improved the overall average score by +5.4 points and showed a substantial gain of +36.3 points on Captioning & Instruction Following, emphasizing the necessity of expressive and task-routed reward functions for diverse answer formats.
*   **SFT vs. RL:** While supervised fine-tuning (SFT) on the Vero-600K dataset yielded performance improvements over a FineVision SFT baseline, reinforcement learning (RL) consistently produced more substantial and uniform gains across all task categories, with an average improvement of +4.8 points over the base model, compared to +0.4 points for SFT.
*   **RL Algorithm:** GSPO (Group Sequence Policy Optimization) achieved the highest average score (54.7) compared to GRPO (54.3) and DAPO (54.3), and maintained more stable policy entropy during training (0.58 ± 0.11), suggesting better exploration and preventing premature policy collapse observed with other algorithms.

**F. Data Diversity and Cross-Task Transfer**
*   **Negative Cross-Task Transfer:** Training models on single task categories frequently resulted in neutral or even negative transfer of performance to non-target tasks. For example, training on non-captioning tasks often severely degraded Captioning & Instruction Following performance.
*   **Elimination of Negative Transfer through Diverse Mixing:** A model trained on a compute-controlled mixture of all task categories achieved positive gains across all categories, effectively eliminating the catastrophic performance losses observed with single-domain training.
*   **Varying Reasoning Lengths:** Different task categories elicited markedly distinct average reasoning lengths (in words). Spatial & Action tasks generated the longest responses (1983 words), while Knowledge & Recognition tasks produced the shortest (75 words), indicating task-specific demands for chain-of-thought reasoning.
*   **Continued Gains with Exposure:** Performance generally improved with greater exposure to the diverse 600K RL training mixture, even within a single training pass, indicating that broad data coverage remains beneficial over longer training durations.

**G. Visual Chat Quality**
*   RL training without explicit open-ended prompts led to significant degradation in visual chat quality, causing models to produce terse, unhelpful answers.
*   The inclusion of the Captioning & Instruction Following task category, along with system prompt guidelines and specific formatting requirements (e.g., `\boxed{}` for definitive answers), and LLM-judge rewards, successfully restored and even improved visual chat quality beyond the base models.
*   Reward hacking, where models generated self-evaluative or fabricated statements to inflate judge scores, was mitigated by incorporating strict "Automatic Failure Conditions" in the LLM judge prompt.

**H. Chain-of-Thought Behaviors**
*   **Distinct Cognitive Profiles:** Analysis of high-level cognitive behaviors revealed that each task category elicited a distinct cognitive behavioral profile. For instance, Captioning involved more mental imagery simulation, Chart tasks triggered systematic regional synthesis, STEM tasks showed elevated backtracking, and Grounding tasks suppressed introspective behaviors in favor of directed visual search.
*   **Task-Specific Skill Repertoires:** A logistic regression probe on extracted low-level skills achieved 0.77 overall accuracy in classifying the task category from reasoning traces, confirming that each category cultivates a largely distinct skill repertoire. Chart and STEM tasks yielded the most distinctive skills, while Knowledge & Recognition was least separable, often confused with Grounding due to shared visual grounding operations. Word clouds of extracted skills further illustrated these domain-specific adaptations (e.g., "apply triangle angle sum" for STEM vs. "locate reference object" for Grounding).

### 6. Significance and Potential Impact

The Vero research contributes to the field of vision-language models by addressing the critical need for transparent, reproducible, and broadly capable RL recipes. Its significance and potential impact can be summarized as follows:

*   **Advocacy for Open Science in Advanced AI:** The work strongly advocates for fully open-source reinforcement learning pipelines for VLMs. By releasing all data, code, and models, Vero demonstrates a commitment to transparency, which is crucial for the scientific community to diagnose model failures, attribute performance gains accurately, and ensure the safety and reliability of advanced AI systems. This fosters a collaborative research environment, moving beyond black-box evaluations prevalent in proprietary systems.

*   **Establishing the Viability of Open Frontier Recipes:** Vero establishes that a transparent, single-stage RL pipeline, when combined with a diverse and carefully curated data mixture and task-routed rewards, can achieve state-of-the-art performance among open-weight models. The finding that Vero can outperform models trained with proprietary RL recipes (e.g., MiMo-VL-7B-RL) is particularly impactful, demonstrating that openness does not necessitate a compromise on performance.

*   **Re-evaluating Multi-Task RL Strategies:** A central finding is the profound importance of data diversity and broad task coverage in multi-task reinforcement learning for visual reasoning. The research demonstrates that diverse task mixing, paired with task-aware reward design, can mitigate common challenges in multi-task learning, such as interference and weak transfer, leading to positive transfer across numerous tasks. This suggests that multi-task RL is as much a problem of *distribution design* as it is of *optimizer design*, echoing lessons from instruction tuning in large language models.

*   **Insights into VLM Cognitive Processes:** The detailed analysis of chain-of-thought behaviors and low-level skills across different task categories provides valuable insights into the emergent cognitive strategies within VLMs. The observation that distinct task categories elicit qualitatively different reasoning patterns (e.g., reflective reasoning for STEM vs. directed visual search for grounding) suggests that VLM reasoning is not monolithic but composed of multiple cognitive-style behaviors adapted to task demands. This aligns with human cognitive science theories on task-switching and cognitive control.

*   **Improving General Visual Chat and Mitigating Reward Hacking:** The methodology for integrating open-ended tasks and using carefully designed LLM-as-judge rewards is significant for maintaining and enhancing visual chat quality during RL. The successful mitigation of reward hacking through strict judge guidelines is a practical contribution that addresses a known challenge in reward-based training.

*   **Foundation for Future Research:** By open-sourcing its components, Vero provides a robust foundation for future research. It encourages further investigation into optimal task taxonomies, the causal mechanisms linking specific behaviors to accuracy, and the application of these open recipes to larger models and more complex multimodal tasks like video understanding or multi-turn interactions.

In conclusion, Vero represents a step toward democratizing high-performance multimodal AI development. It offers a practical and theoretically informed framework for training general visual reasoners with RL, emphasizing transparency, data diversity, and task-specific adaptation as key drivers of advanced capabilities.
overview:
The field of Vision-Language Models (VLMs) has seen substantial progress, with models like Qwen-VL and GPT-4V demonstrating advanced reasoning capabilities across a wide range of visual tasks. While earlier models primarily relied on supervised fine-tuning (SFT) on massive datasets of paired images and text, the most performant recent models have increasingly adopted reinforcement learning (RL) to refine their reasoning processes. These models use techniques such as chain-of-thought (CoT) to "think" through a problem before arriving at an answer, using RL to optimize for correctness and reasoning quality.

However, the state-of-the-art in VLM reasoning is often obscured by proprietary development pipelines. Many frontier models are trained using non-public datasets and undisclosed reward mechanisms, making it difficult for the research community to understand which design choices truly drive performance. Conversely, many existing open-source RL efforts focus on narrow domains like mathematical reasoning, which often fails to generalize to broader visual tasks such as spatial understanding or chart interpretation.

Vero, an initiative from researchers at Princeton University, addresses this gap by providing a fully open RL recipe for general visual reasoning. By releasing the training data, reward code, and model weights, the project aims to establish a transparent framework for building versatile visual reasoners.

![Vero Performance Overview](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.04917v1/x1.png)
*Figure 1: Vero demonstrates improvements across a broad spectrum of visual reasoning tasks, outperforming existing open-weight models and matching proprietary training recipes through its multi-task RL pipeline.*

## Vero-600K: A Diverse Foundation for Visual RL

A primary finding of the Vero project is that training on a single domain—such as visual mathematics—does not reliably improve a model's performance on other visual tasks. In fact, narrow specialization often results in "negative transfer," where the model's performance on general tasks degrades as it over-optimizes for a specific reasoning style.

To solve this, the researchers constructed Vero-600K, a diverse dataset containing 600,000 samples across six core categories:
1.  **STEM:** Reasoning over scientific diagrams, mathematical figures, and medical images.
2.  **Spatial & Action:** Understanding 3D configurations, UI navigation, and planning sequences of actions.
3.  **Knowledge & Recognition:** Combining visual recognition with external knowledge to answer complex queries.
4.  **Chart & OCR:** Extracting and analyzing structured information from charts, tables, and documents.
5.  **Grounding, Counting & Search:** Localizing specific objects, performing visual search, and accurately counting items.
6.  **Captioning & Instruction Following:** Describing images in detail and adhering to specific stylistic or structural instructions.

The dataset was curated from over 250 candidate sources through a rigorous pipeline. Heuristic filtering removed low-resolution images and binary questions (where a model might guess the answer with 50% accuracy). Manual inspection ensured that the remaining samples were unambiguous and verifiable. Finally, large language models (LLMs) were used to normalize ground-truth answers and filter out questions that were irrelevant to the image or poorly phrased.

![Vero-600K Data Distribution](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.04917v1/x2.png)
*Figure 2: The Vero-600K dataset is balanced across six major task categories, ensuring that the model develops a broad repertoire of visual reasoning skills rather than specializing in a single domain.*

## The Reinforcement Learning Recipe

Vero utilizes a single-stage RL pipeline based on Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), which is particularly effective for training models to reason because it allows for comparing multiple outputs for the same prompt. The researchers further refined this into Group Sequence Policy Optimization (GSPO), which stabilizes training by using sequence-level ratios rather than per-token ratios.

The core of the Vero recipe is its "task-routed" reward system. Because visual reasoning tasks are highly heterogeneous, a single reward function (like simple string matching) is insufficient. Vero employs ten distinct reward functions tailored to specific task requirements:
- **String Match and Multiple Choice:** For standard discrete answers.
- **Numeric Verification:** For math and STEM tasks, accounting for precision and formatting.
- **Grounding & Pointing:** Using Intersection over Union (IoU) or distance-based metrics to reward accurate spatial localization.
- **Web Action:** Rewarding the correct sequence of clicks and interactions for UI tasks.
- **LLM-as-Judge:** For open-ended tasks like captioning, a separate judge model (Qwen3-32B) scores responses on a scale from 1 to 10 based on detail, accuracy, and helpfulness.

The total reward $R(y, y^*)$ for a generated response $y$ given the ground truth $y^*$ is calculated as:
$$
R(y, y^*) = R_{\text{acc}}(y, y^*) + R_{\text{fmt}}(y) + R_{\text{overlong}}(y)
$$
where $R_{\text{acc}}$ is the accuracy reward from the task-routed functions, $R_{\text{fmt}}$ penalizes format errors (such as missing the `<think>` or `<answer>` tags), and $R_{\text{overlong}}$ prevents the model from generating unnecessarily long responses.

![Vero Reward Types](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.04917v1/x5.png)
*Figure 3: Overview of the different reward types used in Vero, ranging from programmatic checks for grounding and numbers to LLM-based evaluation for open-ended chat.*

## Performance Across the Visual Reasoning Spectrum

The Vero recipe was applied to several base models, including Qwen3-VL and MiMo-VL. The resulting models, Vero-Qwen3T-8B and Vero-Qwen3I-8B, set a new standard for open 8B-parameter visual reasoners. They achieved consistent improvements across all 30 benchmarks in the VeroEval suite, with notable gains in spatial reasoning and chart analysis.

One significant finding is that Vero training outperformed proprietary RL recipes. For instance, when the Vero recipe was applied to the MiMo-VL base model, it outperformed the official "RL" version of MiMo-VL (trained by the original developers using proprietary data) on several categories, including STEM and Knowledge & Recognition. This demonstrates that a carefully curated open dataset and a multi-route reward system can match or exceed the performance of closed-source pipelines.

![Training Curves](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.04917v1/x6.png)
*Figure 4: RL training curves showing consistent performance gains across all six task categories compared to prior open RL efforts.*

A key challenge in multi-task RL is maintaining "visual chat" ability. Models trained solely on structured tasks (like math or counting) often become unhelpful in open-ended conversation, providing short, robotic answers. By including the "Captioning & Instruction Following" category and using an LLM-as-judge with strict anti-hacking guidelines, Vero models maintain high-quality conversational abilities while simultaneously improving their structured reasoning performance.

## Cognitive Profiles and the Mechanics of Thought

Because Vero models use a thinking-based approach, researchers could analyze the "thinking traces" generated before the final answer. This analysis revealed that the models adapt their cognitive strategies based on the task.

For example, when solving STEM problems, the models exhibit a high degree of "backtracking" and "reflective verification"—checking their work as they proceed through a multi-step calculation. In contrast, for grounding and search tasks, the thinking traces show a pattern of "directed visual search" and "spatial organization," where the model systematically scans regions of the image to find the target object.

The researchers also extracted specific skills from these traces, such as "apply triangle angle sum" or "identify nutrient sources." A classification model could accurately predict the task category simply by looking at the thinking trace, confirming that the RL process cultivates a diverse and domain-specific skill repertoire.

![Reasoning Lengths](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.04917v1/x8.png)
*Figure 5: Different task categories elicit different reasoning behaviors. Spatial and chart tasks typically require significantly longer thinking processes than simple recognition tasks.*

## Ablations and Design Choices

The Vero paper provides several critical insights through ablation studies:
- **RL vs. SFT:** While SFT on the Vero-600K dataset improves performance, RL provides much more substantial and uniform gains across all tasks. RL allows the model to explore different reasoning paths and learn which ones lead to the correct answer, which is more effective than simply mimicking a single demonstration.
- **Task-Routed Rewards:** Using task-specific rewards is essential. A model trained with a unified "one-size-fits-all" reward function (like standard math verification) performed significantly worse, particularly on open-ended tasks and grounding.
- **Data Mixture:** A uniform mixture of all task categories was found to be the most robust. Over-weighting "difficult" tasks (where the base model has low accuracy) often led to instability, as the model struggled to find high-reward samples to learn from.

## Conclusion

Vero demonstrates that general visual reasoning can be effectively improved through a single-stage reinforcement learning pipeline using entirely open resources. By moving beyond narrow specialization and embracing a diverse, multi-task approach with task-specific rewards, the researchers have created a recipe that empowers the broader AI community to build and study advanced multimodal reasoners. The project highlights that performance in modern VLMs is driven as much by the structure of the training distribution and the precision of the reward signals as it is by the underlying architecture. By open-sourcing these components, Vero provides a foundation for more transparent and collaborative research in the next generation of visual-language understanding.

keyInsights: A fully open and transparent RL recipe, leveraging diverse data and task-specific rewards, can achieve performance competitive with or superior to proprietary RL pipelines for general visual reasoning.

Broad data diversity and balanced multi-task mixing are crucial for positive cross-task transfer and preventing catastrophic performance degradation in multi-task reinforcement learning for VLMs, effectively addressing interference challenges.

Carefully designed, task-routed reward functions and strategic inclusion of open-ended tasks are essential for both achieving high accuracy on diverse benchmarks and maintaining/improving general visual chat quality, while mitigating reward hacking.
originalProblem: Most advanced vision-language models (VLMs) rely on proprietary RL pipelines with undisclosed data and reward designs, limiting transparency, analysis, and reproducibility for the research community.
Existing open-source RL efforts for VLMs typically focus on narrow domains (e.g., visual mathematics) and struggle to generalize effectively to a broader range of visual reasoning tasks.
Applying RL across diverse and heterogeneous visual reasoning tasks introduces challenges such as interference, weak skill transfer, and optimization imbalances.
results: Vero models (Vero-Qwen3T-8B, Vero-Qwen3I-8B) achieved state-of-the-art overall average scores (65.9-66.0) among 8B-parameter open-weight VLMs on the 30-benchmark VeroEval suite, surpassing base models on 23-24 out of 30 benchmarks.

Vero training consistently improved performance across four different base models, with average gains ranging from +3.7 to +5.5 points, and Vero-MiMo-7B outperformed a proprietary MiMo-VL-7B-RL on 3 of 6 category averages.

Ablation studies confirmed the superiority of multi-route reward design (+5.4 points overall, +36.3 on Captioning) over unified baselines, and demonstrated RL's more substantial and uniform gains (+4.8 points) compared to SFT (+0.4 points) across task categories.
solution: Developed Vero, a fully open, single-stage reinforcement learning (RL) recipe including code, data (Vero-600K), and models, designed for general visual reasoning across a broad spectrum of tasks.
Curated Vero-600K, a 600,000-sample training dataset derived from 59 distinct datasets, categorized into six broad visual reasoning categories with careful filtering and uniform mixing strategies.

Implemented a multi-faceted reward system utilizing Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) with GSPO advancements, combining task-routed accuracy rewards (ten distinct functions), format penalties, and an overlong penalty, including an LLM-as-judge for open-ended responses.
summary.summary: Princeton University researchers introduce Vero, an open reinforcement learning (RL) framework for training vision-language models (VLMs) that perform general visual reasoning across diverse tasks. Vero models, trained on a 600K diverse dataset and with task-routed rewards, achieved state-of-the-art average scores (e.g., 65.9-66.0) among 8B-parameter open-weight VLMs on a 30-benchmark evaluation suite, consistently outperforming proprietary RL recipes and demonstrating broad improvements across six visual reasoning categories.

title: Vero: An Open RL Recipe for General Visual Reasoning
